text: A forty-two-year-old woman with prior laparoscopic, retrocolic, retrogastric, Roux-en-Y gastric bypass presented with persistent recurrent left upper quadrant pain, diaphoresis, and emesis. Several CTs demonstrated intussusception at the jejuno-jejunostomy. Initial diagnostic laparoscopy confirmed the finding, and enteropexy was performed. However, the patient's symptoms recurred, so revision was recommended. Three five-millimeter ports as well as one twelve-millimeter port were placed through the patient's prior surgical scars. Inspection of the abdomen revealed no adhesions, so the greater omentum was reflected cephalad, and the jejuno-jejunostomy was easily identified. It was clear that this was the site of intermittent intussusception, with the roux limb prolapsing into the common channel. Unfortunately, the interopexy stitch was not effective in preventing this.

The surgeon inspects and traces the small bowel limbs to their origins, identifying the roux limb passing through the mesocolon, the biliopancreatic limb emerging from the ligament of Treitz, and the common channel extending distally to the cecum. The transverse colon defect, Peterson space, and small bowel mesentery are observed to be closed, with no visible signs of obstruction.

The surgeon begins the revision by resecting the jejuno-jejunostomy, using a 60mm endogia stapler with a 2.5mm staple cartridge to transect the biliopancreatic limb, which is marked with a single long suture. The roux limb is similarly transected proximal to the anastomosis and marked with a double suture. The common limb is stapled off, and the mesentery is transected using vascular 2mm reloads. The specimen is set aside for removal in an endocatch bag, leaving the roux limb, common limb, and biliopancreatic limb free.

The surgeon opens the small bowel mesenteric defect using a harmonic scalpel to further mobilize the biliopancreatic limb. A stay suture is placed, and the jejuno-jejunostomy is reconstructed in two steps. A side-to-side functional end-to-end anastomosis is created between the roux limb and the common channel using a triple stapling technique, with two firings of an endo-GIA stapler, each approximately 45 millimeters and 180 degrees apart, followed by closure with a final 60 millimeter endo-GIA with a white load.

The surgeon closes the new mesenteric defect between the root and common limbs using a running 3-0 silk suture. The biliopancreatic limb is then anastomosed to the common limb approximately ten centimeters distal to the first anastomosis. The new mesenteric defect between the biliopancreatic limb and the common limb is also closed with a running 3-0 silk suture. The anatomy is verified from the retrocolic Roux limb to the common channel and anastomosis to the biliopancreatic limb, confirming both anastomoses are patent.
